revision.text:
{{Short description|Mountain in Takamatsu, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2025}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Goken | other_name = 五剣山（ごけんざん） / 八栗山（やくりさん） | photo = | photo_caption = | elevation_m = 375 | country = Japan | region = Shikoku | state = Kagawa Prefecture | location = Takamatsu | map = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_caption = Location in Kagawa Prefecture | listing = }}
'''Mount Goken''' ({{nihongo|'''五剣山'''|Gokenzan|lead=yes}}), also known locally as {{nihongo|'''Yakuriyama'''|八栗山}}, is a 375-metre mountain in [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], Japan. It lies between Mure and Aji districts on the east side of Yashima and is noted for its five rugged peaks, which gave the mountain its name (“five swords”).<ref>{{cite web |title=Mt. Goken |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00409.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism (Official) |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref> The mountain is home to [[Yakuri-ji]] (Temple No. 85 of the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]), which stands on its western slope.<ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuri-ji |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00410.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuri-ji (official site) |url=https://yakuriji.jp/ |website=yakuriji.jp |language=Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>
Due to the potential dangers involved, public access is not typically not available for visitors who wish to climb the mountain's 5 summits.
==Overview== Mount Goken rises above the Sanuki Plain east of Yashima and looks out over the [[Seto Inland Sea]]. The mountain’s distinctive summit ridge was historically described as five sword-like peaks; local tradition holds that one peak collapsed during the 1707 earthquake, giving the ridge its present outline.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mt. Goken |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00409.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref> The mountain’s elevation is commonly given as 375 m.<ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism (Official) |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>
The area is associated with the quarrying of high-quality “Aji stone” granite, prized by sculptors and builders and worked for centuries in nearby Aji.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mt. Goken |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00409.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>
==Yakuri-ji== {{main|Yakuri-ji}} [[File:|thumb|right|upright=1.1|Yakuri-ji is situated partway up Mount Goken.]] [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') is a [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] temple founded, according to temple tradition, by [[Kūkai]] in 829. It is Temple No. 85 on the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuri-ji (official site) |url=https://yakuriji.jp/ |website=yakuriji.jp |language=Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuri-ji |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00410.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref> The temple complex stands partway up the mountain (often described as around the “8th station”) and is reached by hiking trails or by the Yakuri Cable funicular from Mure.<ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism (Official) |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuri-ji |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00410.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>
==Etymology and traditions== The name “Goken” (“five swords”) refers to the serrated skyline of the summit ridge; a related legend ties the name to five swords said to have descended from the heavens during Kūkai’s practices on the mountain. Because of the temple’s importance, the mountain is also widely known as “Yakuriyama.”<ref>{{cite web |title=Mt. Goken |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00409.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism (Official) |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage – Temple 85 Yakuriji |url=https://shikokupilgrimage.news/en/shikoku-88-temples/85-yakuriji-temple |website=Shikoku Pilgrimage News |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>
==Access and views== Trails ascend the mountain from the surrounding neighborhoods, and the Yakuri Cable provides a quick approach to Yakuri-ji near the mountain’s midpoint. From the temple precincts there are broad views over the Seto Inland Sea; the serrated peaks of Mount Goken are also prominent when seen from the top of nearby Yashima.<ref>{{cite web |title=Mt. Goken |url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00409.html |website=Japan Tourism Agency (Tagengo-DB) |language=English/Japanese |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism (Official) |access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Yashima]] *[[Shikoku Pilgrimage]] *[[List of mountains and hills of Japan by height]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== *{{official|https://yakuriji.jp/|Yakuri-ji (official site) (Japanese)}} *[https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/R3-00409.html Mt. Goken – Japan Tourism Agency (multilingual)] *[https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 Temple 85, Yakuriji – Shikoku Tourism (official)]
{{Authority control}} [[Category:Mountains of Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Takamatsu, Kagawa]] [[Category:Shikoku Pilgrimage]] [[Category:Sacred mountains of Japan]]

{{Short description|Mountain in Takamatsu, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=November 2025}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Goken | other_name = {{lang|ja|五剣山}} / ''Gokenzan''; also called {{lang|ja|八栗山}} ''Yakuriyama'' | photo = | photo_caption = | elevation_m = 375 | prominence_m = | listing = | location = [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | range = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|40|N|134|08|29|E|display=inline,title}} | map = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_caption = Location in Kagawa Prefecture | label = Mount Goken | easiest_route = By [[Yakuri-ji|Yakuri]] cable car and footpath }}
'''Mount Goken''' (Japanese: {{nihongo|五剣山|Gokenzan}}, lit. ''Five Swords Mountain''), also known locally as {{nihongo|八栗山|Yakuriyama}}, is a 375 m mountain in [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], Japan. It lies within [[Setonaikai National Park]] and is noted for five steep crags (traditionally counted as “five swords”) and for [[Yakuri-ji]], Temple 85 of the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]], on its western slope.<ref name="ja">{{cite web |title=五剣山 (高松市の山) |url=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%94%E5%89%A3%E5%B1%B1_%28%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%B8%82%E3%81%AE%E5%B1%B1%29 |website=Japanese Wikipedia |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref name="YakuriSite">{{cite web |title=第85番札所 五剣山観自在院 八栗寺 |url=https://yakuriji.jp/ |website=Yakuriji official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Setonaikai National Park |url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setonaikai_National_Park |website=English Wikipedia |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Overview == From the [[Sanuki Plain]] the mountain’s skyline is popularly compared to the profile of a reclining Buddha. Local tradition associated with [[Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) holds that five swords descended from heaven during his ascetic training here, giving the mountain its name; another etymology derives from its five major pinnacles, counted from west to east as the First through Fifth “Sword.”<ref name="ja" />
The highest point (375 m) is on the Fourth Sword. A 366 m summit mark stands on the Second Sword, and a fourth-order triangulation point (366.09 m) lies at the west end of the First Sword.<ref name="ja" /> Several small shrines are located on the ridgeline; an intermediate cave on the Fourth Sword has long served as a site for [[Shugendō]] practice.<ref name="ja" />
== Geology and hazards == Mount Goken’s pinnacles consist mainly of volcanic breccia of the Miocene Sanuki Group with weaker interbedded tuff layers. During the [[1707 Hōei earthquake]], part of the easternmost peak (the Fifth Sword) collapsed as large rockfalls, altering the mountain’s outline; strong horizontal accelerations (estimated ~300–500 gal) have been inferred from historical sources and field evidence.<ref>{{cite web |title=History of Disasters – Mt. Goken (Mt. Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
Granite known as '''Aji stone''' ({{lang|ja|庵治石}}), quarried at the foot of the mountain, is prized as a high-grade stone for monuments and architecture, including use at Japan’s National Diet Building.<ref name="ja" />
== Religion == [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') stands on the mountain’s western slope and serves as the 85th temple of the Shikoku Pilgrimage. Temple traditions relate that Kūkai practiced the [[Kokūzō gumonjihō]] here and named the mountain after burying five swords; the site later functioned as a prayer temple for the Takamatsu domain. The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Yakuri-ji]] * [[Setonaikai National Park]] * [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://yakuriji.jp/}} {{lang|ja}} * {{Commons category-inline|Gokenzan (Takamatsu City)}}
[[Category:Mountains of Kagawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Takamatsu]] [[Category:Setonaikai National Park]]

Goken (Mt. Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>

The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>

{{Short description|Mountain in Takamatsu, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=November 2025}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Goken | other_name = {{lang|ja|五剣山}} / ''Gokenzan''; also called {{lang|ja|八栗山}} ''Yakuriyama'' | photo = | photo_caption = | elevation_m = 375 | prominence_m = | listing = | location = [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | range = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|40|N|134|08|29|E|display=inline,title}} | map = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_caption = Location in Kagawa Prefecture | label = Mount Goken | easiest_route = By [[Yakuri-ji|Yakuri]] cable car and footpath }}

{{Short description|Mountain in Takamatsu, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=November 2025}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Goken | other_name = {{lang|ja|五剣山}} / ''Gokenzan''; also called {{lang|ja|八栗山}} ''Yakuriyama'' | photo = | photo_caption = | elevation_m = 375 | prominence_m = | listing = | location = [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | range = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|40|N|134|08|29|E|display=inline,title}} | map = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_caption = Location in Kagawa Prefecture | label = Mount Goken | easiest_route = By [[Yakuri-ji|Yakuri]] cable car and footpath }}
'''Mount Goken''' (Japanese: {{nihongo|五剣山|Gokenzan}}, lit. ''Five Swords Mountain''), also known locally as {{nihongo|八栗山|Yakuriyama}}, is a 375 m mountain in [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], Japan. It lies within [[Setonaikai National Park]] and is noted for five steep crags (traditionally counted as “five swords”) and for [[Yakuri-ji]], Temple 85 of the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]], on its western slope.<ref name="ja">{{cite web |title=五剣山 (高松市の山) |url=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%94%E5%89%A3%E5%B1%B1_%28%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%B8%82%E3%81%AE%E5%B1%B1%29 |website=Japanese Wikipedia |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref name="YakuriSite">{{cite web |title=第85番札所 五剣山観自在院 八栗寺 |url=https://yakuriji.jp/ |website=Yakuriji official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Setonaikai National Park |url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setonaikai_National_Park |website=English Wikipedia |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Overview == From the [[Sanuki Plain]] the mountain’s skyline is popularly compared to the profile of a reclining Buddha. Local tradition associated with [[Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) holds that five swords descended from heaven during his ascetic training here, giving the mountain its name; another etymology derives from its five major pinnacles, counted from west to east as the First through Fifth “Sword.”<ref name="ja" />
The highest point (375 m) is on the Fourth Sword. A 366 m summit mark stands on the Second Sword, and a fourth-order triangulation point (366.09 m) lies at the west end of the First Sword.<ref name="ja" /> Several small shrines are located on the ridgeline; an intermediate cave on the Fourth Sword has long served as a site for [[Shugendō]] practice.<ref name="ja" />
== Geology and hazards == Mount Goken’s pinnacles consist mainly of volcanic breccia of the Miocene Sanuki Group with weaker interbedded tuff layers. During the [[1707 Hōei earthquake]], part of the easternmost peak (the Fifth Sword) collapsed as large rockfalls, altering the mountain’s outline; strong horizontal accelerations (estimated ~300–500 gal) have been inferred from historical sources and field evidence.<ref>{{cite web |title=History of Disasters – Mt. Goken (Mt. Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
Granite known as '''Aji stone''' ({{lang|ja|庵治石}}), quarried at the foot of the mountain, is prized as a high-grade stone for monuments and architecture, including use at Japan’s National Diet Building.<ref name="ja" />
== Religion == [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') stands on the mountain’s western slope and serves as the 85th temple of the Shikoku Pilgrimage. Temple traditions relate that Kūkai practiced the [[Kokūzō gumonjihō]] here and named the mountain after burying five swords; the site later functioned as a prayer temple for the Takamatsu domain. The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Yakuri-ji]] * [[Setonaikai National Park]] * [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://yakuriji.jp/}} {{lang|ja}} * {{Commons category-inline|Gokenzan (Takamatsu City)}}

Local tradition associated with [[Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) holds that five swords descended from heaven during his ascetic training here, giving the mountain its name; another etymology derives from its five major pinnacles, counted from west to east as the First through Fifth “Sword.”<ref name="ja" />

A 366 m summit mark stands on the Second Sword, and a fourth-order triangulation point (366.09 m) lies at the west end of the First Sword.<ref name="ja" /> Several small shrines are located on the ridgeline; an intermediate cave on the Fourth Sword has long served as a site for [[Shugendō]] practice.<ref name="ja" />
== Geology and hazards == Mount Goken’s pinnacles consist mainly of volcanic breccia of the Miocene Sanuki Group with weaker interbedded tuff layers. During the [[1707 Hōei earthquake]], part of the easternmost peak (the Fifth Sword) collapsed as large rockfalls, altering the mountain’s outline; strong horizontal accelerations (estimated ~300–500 gal) have been inferred from historical sources and field evidence.<ref>{{cite web |title=History of Disasters – Mt. Goken (Mt. Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
Granite known as '''Aji stone''' ({{lang|ja|庵治石}}), quarried at the foot of the mountain, is prized as a high-grade stone for monuments and architecture, including use at Japan’s National Diet Building.<ref name="ja" />
== Religion == [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') stands on the mountain’s western slope and serves as the 85th temple of the Shikoku Pilgrimage. Temple traditions relate that Kūkai practiced the [[Kokūzō gumonjihō]] here and named the mountain after burying five swords; the site later functioned as a prayer temple for the Takamatsu domain. The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>

'''Mount Goken''' (Japanese: {{nihongo|五剣山|Gokenzan}}, lit. ''Five Swords Mountain''), also known locally as {{nihongo|八栗山|Yakuriyama}}, is a 375 m mountain in [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], Japan. It lies within [[Setonaikai National Park]] and is noted for five steep crags (traditionally counted as “five swords”) and for [[Yakuri-ji]], Temple 85 of the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]], on its western slope.<ref name="ja">{{cite web |title=五剣山 (高松市の山) |url=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%94%E5%89%A3%E5%B1%B1_%28%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%B8%82%E3%81%AE%E5%B1%B1%29 |website=Japanese Wikipedia |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref name="YakuriSite">{{cite web |title=第85番札所 五剣山観自在院 八栗寺 |url=https://yakuriji.jp/ |website=Yakuriji official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Setonaikai National Park |url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setonaikai_National_Park |website=English Wikipedia |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>

A 366 m summit mark stands on the Second Sword, and a fourth-order triangulation point (366.09 m) lies at the west end of the First Sword.<ref name="ja" /> Several small shrines are located on the ridgeline; an intermediate cave on the Fourth Sword has long served as a site for [[Shugendō]] practice.<ref name="ja" />
== Geology and hazards == Mount Goken’s pinnacles consist mainly of volcanic breccia of the Miocene Sanuki Group with weaker interbedded tuff layers. During the [[1707 Hōei earthquake]], part of the easternmost peak (the Fifth Sword) collapsed as large rockfalls, altering the mountain’s outline; strong horizontal accelerations (estimated ~300–500 gal) have been inferred from historical sources and field evidence.<ref>{{cite web |title=History of Disasters – Mt. Goken (Mt. Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
Granite known as '''Aji stone''' ({{lang|ja|庵治石}}), quarried at the foot of the mountain, is prized as a high-grade stone for monuments and architecture, including use at Japan’s National Diet Building.<ref name="ja" />
== Religion == [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') stands on the mountain’s western slope and serves as the 85th temple of the Shikoku Pilgrimage. Temple traditions relate that Kūkai practiced the [[Kokūzō gumonjihō]] here and named the mountain after burying five swords; the site later functioned as a prayer temple for the Takamatsu domain. The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>

Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
Granite known as '''Aji stone''' ({{lang|ja|庵治石}}), quarried at the foot of the mountain, is prized as a high-grade stone for monuments and architecture, including use at Japan’s National Diet Building.<ref name="ja" />
== Religion == [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') stands on the mountain’s western slope and serves as the 85th temple of the Shikoku Pilgrimage. Temple traditions relate that Kūkai practiced the [[Kokūzō gumonjihō]] here and named the mountain after burying five swords; the site later functioned as a prayer temple for the Takamatsu domain. The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Mountain in Takamatsu, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=November 2025}} {{Infobox mountain | name = Mount Goken | other_name = {{lang|ja|五剣山}} / ''Gokenzan''; also called {{lang|ja|八栗山}} ''Yakuriyama'' | photo = | photo_caption = | elevation_m = 375 | prominence_m = | listing = | location = [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | range = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|40|N|134|08|29|E|display=inline,title}} | map = Japan Kagawa Prefecture#Japan | map_caption = Location in Kagawa Prefecture | label = Mount Goken | easiest_route = By [[Yakuri-ji|Yakuri]] cable car and footpath }}
'''Mount Goken''' (Japanese: {{nihongo|五剣山|Gokenzan}}, lit. ''Five Swords Mountain''), also known locally as {{nihongo|八栗山|Yakuriyama}}, is a 375 m mountain in [[Takamatsu]], [[Kagawa Prefecture]], Japan. It lies within [[Setonaikai National Park]] and is noted for five steep crags (traditionally counted as “five swords”) and for [[Yakuri-ji]], Temple 85 of the [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]], on its western slope.<ref name="ja">{{cite web |title=五剣山 (高松市の山) |url=https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%94%E5%89%A3%E5%B1%B1_%28%E9%AB%98%E6%9D%BE%E5%B8%82%E3%81%AE%E5%B1%B1%29 |website=Japanese Wikipedia |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref name="YakuriSite">{{cite web |title=第85番札所 五剣山観自在院 八栗寺 |url=https://yakuriji.jp/ |website=Yakuriji official site |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Temple 85, Yakuriji｜What to See & Do |url=https://shikoku-tourism.com/en/see-and-do/10208 |website=Shikoku Tourism |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Setonaikai National Park |url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setonaikai_National_Park |website=English Wikipedia |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Overview == From the [[Sanuki Plain]] the mountain’s skyline is popularly compared to the profile of a reclining Buddha. Local tradition associated with [[Kūkai]] (Kōbō Daishi) holds that five swords descended from heaven during his ascetic training here, giving the mountain its name; another etymology derives from its five major pinnacles, counted from west to east as the First through Fifth “Sword.”<ref name="ja" />
The highest point (375 m) is on the Fourth Sword. A 366 m summit mark stands on the Second Sword, and a fourth-order triangulation point (366.09 m) lies at the west end of the First Sword.<ref name="ja" /> Several small shrines are located on the ridgeline; an intermediate cave on the Fourth Sword has long served as a site for [[Shugendō]] practice.<ref name="ja" />
== Geology and hazards == Mount Goken’s pinnacles consist mainly of volcanic breccia of the Miocene Sanuki Group with weaker interbedded tuff layers. During the [[1707 Hōei earthquake]], part of the easternmost peak (the Fifth Sword) collapsed as large rockfalls, altering the mountain’s outline; strong horizontal accelerations (estimated ~300–500 gal) have been inferred from historical sources and field evidence.<ref>{{cite web |title=History of Disasters – Mt. Goken (Mt. Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
Granite known as '''Aji stone''' ({{lang|ja|庵治石}}), quarried at the foot of the mountain, is prized as a high-grade stone for monuments and architecture, including use at Japan’s National Diet Building.<ref name="ja" />
== Religion == [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') stands on the mountain’s western slope and serves as the 85th temple of the Shikoku Pilgrimage. Temple traditions relate that Kūkai practiced the [[Kokūzō gumonjihō]] here and named the mountain after burying five swords; the site later functioned as a prayer temple for the Takamatsu domain. The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Yakuri-ji]] * [[Setonaikai National Park]] * [[Shikoku Pilgrimage]]
== References == {{reflist}}
== External links == * {{Official website|https://yakuriji.jp/}} {{lang|ja}} * {{Commons category-inline|Gokenzan (Takamatsu City)}}

During the [[1707 Hōei earthquake]], part of the easternmost peak (the Fifth Sword) collapsed as large rockfalls, altering the mountain’s outline; strong horizontal accelerations (estimated ~300–500 gal) have been inferred from historical sources and field evidence.<ref>{{cite web |title=History of Disasters – Mt. Goken (Mt. Yaguri) |url=https://www.kagawa-u.ac.jp/iecms_english/history_and_culture_of_kagawa/history_of_disasters.html |website=Kagawa University |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=1707年宝永地震による讃岐五剣山の山体崩壊 |url=https://www.jsce.or.jp/library/eq10/proc/00035/58-03-0197.pdf |website=Japan Society of Civil Engineers |language=ja |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=宝永4年の地震による五剣山の崩壊 |url=https://www.bousai.go.jp/kyoiku/kyokun/kyoukunnokeishou/rep/1707_houeijishin/pdf/08_chap04.pdf |website=Cabinet Office, Government of Japan (Disaster Management) |language=ja |date=2014 |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
Granite known as '''Aji stone''' ({{lang|ja|庵治石}}), quarried at the foot of the mountain, is prized as a high-grade stone for monuments and architecture, including use at Japan’s National Diet Building.<ref name="ja" />
== Religion == [[Yakuri-ji]] (formally ''Gokenzan Kanjizai-in Yakuri-ji'') stands on the mountain’s western slope and serves as the 85th temple of the Shikoku Pilgrimage. Temple traditions relate that Kūkai practiced the [[Kokūzō gumonjihō]] here and named the mountain after burying five swords; the site later functioned as a prayer temple for the Takamatsu domain. The precincts include a cable-car upper station and viewpoints over the [[Seto Inland Sea]].<ref name="YakuriSite" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Gokenzan Kanjizaiin Yakuriji – Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage |url=https://88shikokuhenro.jp/en/gokenzan-kanjizaiin-yakuriji/ |website=Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage (official) |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
== Access == * By road from [[Japan National Route 11|National Route 11]] to Kagawa Prefectural Route 145 (Yakuri Shindō), then approximately 3 km to trailheads.<ref name="ja" /> * By the '''Yakuri Cable''' to Yakuri-Sanjo Station; about 5 minutes on foot to the temple precincts and mountain paths.<ref name="ja" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Yakuriji Temple Travel Guides |url=https://japantravel.navitime.com/en/area/jp/spot/02301-2800164/ |website=Japan Travel by NAVITIME |language=en |access-date=2025-11-10}}</ref>
